note_data.title: Weather
keyPoints: **Weather** is the day-to-day condition of the atmosphere at a particular place and time, including temperature, rainfall, wind speed and direction, humidity and cloud cover
**Temperature** is measured using a thermometer in degrees Celsius, recorded in a Stevenson Screen to ensure accurate readings away from direct sunlight
**Rainfall** is measured using a rain gauge and recorded in millimetres, with the gauge placed away from buildings and trees to avoid interference
**Wind direction** is measured using a wind vane and shows where the wind is coming from, while **wind speed** is measured using an anemometer in kilometres per hour
**Cloud cover** is measured in oktas (eighths of sky covered) and affects temperature by blocking incoming solar radiation during day and trapping heat at night
Weather instruments must be placed in a **Stevenson Screen** which is painted white, has louvred sides, raised 1.2m above ground, and faces away from the sun
**Diurnal temperature range** is the difference between maximum and minimum temperature in 24 hours and varies with season, latitude and proximity to sea
Coastal areas like Karachi have **smaller diurnal temperature ranges** because water heats and cools more slowly than land
**Prevailing winds** are the most common wind direction in an area and significantly influence local weather patterns and rainfall distribution

definitions.definition: The day-to-day condition of the atmosphere at a particular place and time
The average weather conditions of a place recorded over a period of at least 30 years
A white wooden box with louvred sides, raised 1.2m above ground, used to house weather instruments and protect them from direct sunlight and precipitation
The difference between the maximum and minimum temperature recorded in a 24-hour period
The most frequent wind direction recorded at a weather station over a period of time

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Say what you can see in data/diagram. Use figures from the resource.
State similarities AND differences using comparative words like 'whereas', 'however', 'both'

modelAnswer.question: Explain why coastal areas have smaller diurnal temperature ranges than inland areas. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Water has a high specific heat capacity so it heats up and cools down more slowly than land. During the day, the sea remains cooler than the land, so onshore breezes reduce maximum temperatures. At night, the sea remains warmer than the land, so it prevents temperatures from falling too low. This moderating effect of the sea reduces the difference between maximum and minimum temperatures in coastal areas.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Water heats up and cools down slowly/high specific heat capacity
Mark 2: Sea cooler during day/onshore breeze reduces maximum temperature
Mark 3: Sea warmer at night/prevents low temperatures
Mark 4: Overall moderating effect reduces temperature range

commonMistakes: Never say 'climate changes daily' — weather changes daily, climate is long-term average
Students often confuse wind direction - winds are named after where they come from, not where they go to
Don't say thermometer measures 'heat' — it measures temperature in degrees Celsius
Never place weather instruments in direct sunlight or near buildings — this gives inaccurate readings
Students often forget that Stevenson Screen must be painted white to reflect heat and prevent overheating of instruments
examinerTips: Weather appears frequently in Paper 2 with data interpretation questions
Always use figures from weather data tables when describing patterns
Draw and label diagrams of weather instruments for full marks in equipment questions
Remember the difference between weather and climate - examiners test this regularly
formulas: Diurnal temperature range = Maximum temperature - Minimum temperature
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Stevenson Screen diagram showing white painted wooden box with louvred sides, door opening away from sun, raised 1.2m above ground on legs, containing thermometers and other weather instruments
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (6 marks) — they asked students to draw a labelled diagram explaining freeze-thaw weathering with water entering cracks
Physical weathering processes appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q4 (4 marks) — candidates had to explain how temperature affects weathering rates
Chemical weathering was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q2 (5 marks) — students needed to explain the role of climate in determining weathering rates
Root action weathering was tested in O/N 2023 P1 Q3 (3 marks) — requiring identification and explanation of physical weathering processes
Temperature fluctuation effects appeared in M/J 2022 P2 Q4 (4 marks) — linking heating and cooling cycles to rock breakdown

workedExample.question: Explain how temperature can affect the type and rate of weathering (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that temperature affects both physical and chemical weathering processes
Step 2: Explain freeze-thaw weathering - water enters cracks, freezes at 0°C, expands by 9%, creates pressure
Step 3: Describe heating and cooling cycles - rocks expand when heated, contract when cooled, causing stress fractures
Step 4: Link to chemical weathering - higher temperatures increase chemical reaction rates, making chemical weathering faster
workedExample.answer: Temperature affects weathering through freeze-thaw cycles where water enters rock cracks, freezes and expands by 9%, creating pressure that splits the rock. Daily heating and cooling causes rocks to expand and contract repeatedly, creating stress fractures. Higher temperatures also increase the rate of chemical reactions, making chemical weathering processes like carbonation more effective in breaking down rocks.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for freeze-thaw process, 1 mark for expansion/pressure explanation, 1 mark for heating/cooling cycles, 1 mark for chemical weathering rate increase

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused weathering with erosion' — always remember weathering breaks down rocks in situ (in place) while erosion involves transport
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students often failed to explain the 9% expansion of water when freezing' — always mention specific expansion percentage for freeze-thaw weathering
Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates gave vague answers about temperature effects' — be specific about daily heating/cooling cycles and seasonal freeze-thaw patterns
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw a freeze-thaw weathering sequence showing: 1) Intact rock with hairline crack, 2) Water entering the crack during day/warm period, 3) Ice formation at night/cold period with expanded crack (show 9% expansion), 4) Repeated cycles leading to rock splitting completely. Label: water, ice, crack expansion, pressure arrows, and temperature conditions. Include cross-sectional view showing progressive widening of cracks.
note_data.quickRecap: Weathering breaks rocks down in place through physical (freeze-thaw, heating/cooling, root action) and chemical processes. Freeze-thaw occurs when water expands 9% upon freezing in rock cracks. Temperature fluctuations cause expansion and contraction stress. Climate determines weathering rates - more water and temperature changes mean faster weathering.
